@ladygaga attended the premiere of ‘Mayhem REQUIEM’ in Los Angeles wearing an archival @givenchy by @alexandermcqueen AW1997 Haute Couture ensemble from the legendary “Eclect Dissect” collection, inspired by Spanish bullfighting.
Originally designed during McQueen’s early years at Givenchy, the dramatic look combines scarlet satin, black lace, embellished matador shoulders, and a sweeping lace train, bringing one of fashion history’s most theatrical couture moments back to life.
More than a vintage reference, Gaga once again proves how archival fashion can still feel emotional, dangerous, and completely alive nearly three decades later.
Words by Josephine Jiang

posts.caption:
After graduating from the Art Center College of Design in Pasadena, California, September 1964, photographer @gideonlewin travelled to New York to interview for a job with Richard Avedon, who was born on this day in 1923. He arrived at 110 East 58th Street with a leatherbound portfolio in hand and took the elevator up to the fourth floor. Just as he was about to enter Avedon’s studio, the famed model Wilhelmina breezed out. “I knew I was in the right place,” Lewin tells AnOther.⁠
⁠
That auspicious encounter marked the start of a 16-year partnership poignantly chronicled in the book Avedon – Behind the Scenes 1964-1980, a lavish monograph featuring intimate stories and behind-the-scenes photographs from some of the best moments in Avedon’s career. Whether photographing legends like Sophia Loren, Audrey Hepburn, Cher, Veruschka, or Twiggy, Lewin paints a revealing portrait of Avedon, the artist and the man. ⁠
⁠
At the link in bio, Lewin shares his memories of what it was like to work side-by-side with one of the greatest photographers of the 20th century 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Avedon – Behind the Scenes 1964-1980. From ​Avedon​ by Gideon Lewin, published by powerHouse Books⁠
⁠
#RichardAvedon

The @loewefoundation Craft Prize has landed in Singapore for its latest edition, naming Korean ceramicist @jongjinpark_ceramics its winner. As an exhibition of the finalists’ works opens, we present some of the most fascinating works. ⁠

More than 5,100 submissions arrived from 133 countries and regions for the 2026 edition in Singapore, though only 30 finalists made the cut, selected by a panel of jurors that, this year, includes @jackmccollough and @lazro for the first time. “The deliberation process was really difficult,” says McCollough. “There were some heated discussions, but we’re super happy with the final result.” ⁠

posts.caption:
Madonna at the 1991 Cannes Film Festival for ‘Truth or Dare’ premiere wearing Jean Paul Gaultier.
On May 13 1991, Madonna attended the premiere of Truth Or Dare at the Cannes Film Festival.  In Europe the documentary was re-titled In Bed With Madonna. She wore a Jean Paul Gaultier-designed pink satin cone bra and briefs with a matching robe.
Gaultier took a vintage, 1950s style and made it look futuristic and bold. This moment helped define his career as a designer who loved to break rules and mix “trashy” elements with luxury fashion.

posts.caption:
Over her stellar 20-year career, @phoebejtonkin has captivated audiences. The Sydney-born, New York-based actor began acting as a teenager playing a mermaid in the series ‘H2O: Just Add Water,’ and has since achieved a dynamic array of dramatic parts, a stint in the director’s chair and a new role producing and starring in the adaptation of crime thriller, ‘The Dark Lake’. While she’s relishing the collaborative process, the actor and May 2026 cover star tells BAZAAR, acting remains her first love.“I feel really lucky to be invited into the collaborative process so early on in a project. Usually, as an actor, you turn up and you’re told, ‘This is what your set looks like, these are your fellow actors’. And with this, I get to be there from the inception of the project and be part of these decisions.”
Through the link in bio, Phoebe Tonkin reflects on her career and attraction to robust roles that “lean into the complexities of being a woman.”

posts.caption:
“I’m attracted to things that are authentic and grounded,” @phoebejtonkin tells BAZAAR of her latest role in the cross-genre ‘Two Years Later’, a post-pandemic romantic dramedy set in Brisbane. Tonkin plays Emily, a woman with a complicated love and family life who sets out on a series of dates with Ryan, played by Brenton Thwaites, to work out if they’re destined to marry. “There was something quite relatable and ordinary about these two people who are in their 30s and trying to navigate dating, coming out of a pandemic, all the isolation and loneliness that they’ve been dealing with. And I just thought it was really real,” she shares. It was this funny, quirky love story drama that you don’t get to do often.”
Continue reading Phoebe Tonkin’s cover story through the link in bio.

posts.caption:
In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.
Read the full cover story at the link in bio.

#BazaarOnStands: Rasika Dugal (@rasikadugal) has built a body of work that lives beyond the glamour of screens, rooted instead in craft, restraint, and emotional precision. From the moral intensity of ‘Delhi Crime’, to the smouldering quiet of Beena Tripathi in ‘Mirzapur’, and the layered vulnerability of ‘Out of Love’, her performances linger for their honesty rather than volume. It is this deep respect for storytelling—and a curiosity for the unfamiliar—that shapes her choices, keeping her deliberately selective as she makes space for more women-led narratives that continue to evolve, still very much in the making.

After queuing for days to get their hands on the new Audemars Piguet-Swatch collaboration, fans on Saturday finally got their chance to buy the eye-catching pop-art timepiece, but heaving crowds stopped some stores from opening their doors and prompted pleas for calm.
Such was the frenzy for the timepiece at the Dubai Mall and the Mall of the Emirates in Dubai that the launch was canceled over crowd safety concerns, Swatch said on its Instagram account. Similar scenes played out in the UK, France, US and Singapore.
“To ensure the safety of both our customers and our staff in Swatch stores, we kindly ask you not to rush to our stores in large numbers to acquire this product,” the company said in a statement on its Instagram account, adding that the new timepieces will be available for several months.
Read the full story #linkinbio

posts.caption:
For the global luxury industry, the last two years have been defined by a prolonged period of meagre growth, macro-uncertainty, and a slow recovery in the critical Chinese market. But as we move further into 2026, the strategic imperative has shifted. It is no longer enough to simply wait for the cycle to turn; leadership now requires navigating a rapidly-changing environment where geopolitical volatility and technological disruption have become the baseline.
In this episode of The BoF Podcast, Jonathan Wingfield (@misterwingfield), editor-in-chief of System Magazine joins Imran Amed (@imranamed) and Luca Solca, managing director and global head of luxury goods at Bernstein, for their regular seasonal conversation on the state of the industry. They analyse this new industry paradigm through two distinct lenses: the clinical, data-driven reality of the equity markets, and the visceral, creative pulse of culture. They examining the collapse of the old narrative within luxury, why brand heat has become a lazy currency, and why the real threat of AI isn’t the technology itself, but the professionals who master it first.

posts.caption:
In fashion today, dupes are a standard practice. Its practitioners aren’t just the typical culprits of fast fashion brands and anonymous Amazon sellers; major retailers and even luxury brands all engage in some form of “duping.”
This reality has created a new push-pull dynamic between influencers and brands. While sharing dupe versions or creating their own is a near-certain financial win, influencers risk upsetting the brands they hope to partner with.
Read the full story on how to navigate the tricky business of dupes, including what the best defense is for brands #linkinbio

posts.caption:
LVMH announced on Thursday that it is selling Marc Jacobs to brand management firm WHP Global. Financial details of the sale were not disclosed.
Marc Jacobs will continue in his role as creative director.
“I am forever grateful to Bernard Arnault for his support, belief and trust in me over the last 30 years,” Jacobs said in a statement. “It has been an honor and privilege to work alongside the Arnault Family and LVMH. I remain committed in my role as Creative Director ... and look forward to this bright new chapter.”
The move confirms reports in the Wall Street Journal and others that the French luxury conglomerate was moving ahead with a long-studied sale of Marc Jacobs, targeting a $1 billion valuation.
Read the full story by BoF’s @robert.williams.writes #linkinbio

posts.caption:
While nuptials of the rich and famous have long been attention fodder, patronage by brands was usually reserved to the likes of A-listers like Kourtney Kardashian, whose 2022 wedding to Travis Barker “hosted” by Dolce & Gabbana drove $10.8 million in earned media value for the fashion house, according to influencer marketing platform CreatorIQ.
In 2026, interest has reached a new high — and expanded well beyond celebrities. Influencers and even ordinary brides have realised that their bridal journeys are attention bait and are documenting accordingly, landing partnerships along the way.
BoF’s Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) explores how brands, stylists and publicists are cashing in as weddings become content productions with viral potential. Read more #linkinbio

posts.caption: this didn’t feel like a shock tactic collection, which is probably why it landed better. there’s definitely a balenciaga ghost in there, especially in the oversized tailoring, the sharp black looks and those huge silhouettes, but it never fully loses gucci’s own language. he whole “gucci core” thing is very internet-savvy, very gen z, very now. and the styling had real energy. my fav look with the blue shirt, grey fur and pink flowers, felt surprisingly human. but everything about this collection feels cold, expensive, detached and deeply aware of status. the dramatic fur, severe eyewear and 90s nocturnal glamour all point towards fashion moving away from quiet luxury and back into loud city glam. this isn’t about looking tasteful anymore. it’s about looking pretentiously unreachable and obviously expensive. paris hilton as a brunette was a great twist, tom brady in the black leather look felt perfectly cast. and the makeup gave everything this glamorous, exhausted edge. commercial, yes, but still interesting. he is selling to the new kids and he is sure of it [fortunately or unfortunately is a call you gotta take]

posts.caption:
Questions around what to expect at the 2026 Cannes Film Festival have so far largely focused on two things: The Hollywood-shaped hole in the official selection and “The White Lotus,” which is currently shooting its fourth season on location at the festival.
This year, the storied celebration of film held on the French Riviera will look a little less “Mission: Impossible,” which premiered on the Croisette in 2025, and a little more international as European and Asian directors such as Pedro Almodóvar, Nicholas Winding Refn and Ryusuke Hamaguchi take center stage.
But Tuesday’s opening night of the festival proved that the red carpet will not have that same Hollywood-shaped hole. The fashion is already as glittering as ever, with jury member @demimoore, along with fellow actors @janefonda and @maikamonroe all arriving for opening night dripping in sequins.
Tap the link in bio for more as Cannes Film Festival continues.
📸: Ming Yeung/Getty Images/Doug Peters/PA Wire/Mike Marsland/WireImage/Andreas Rentz/Aurore Marechal/Stephane Cardinale/Corbis/Gisela Schober/German Select/Valery Hache/AFP/Kate Green/Amy Sussman/Manon Cruz/Reuters

posts.caption:
The Gold Gala (@goldhouseco) held on Saturday in Los Angeles is a celebration of Asian Pacific talent — and the red carpet is often a visual extension of that.
Many stars wore looks leaning into their cultural heritage and by Asian designers. Actor @priyankachopra wore custom Amit Aggarwal (@amitaggarwalofficial) — which showcased the delicate Chikankari embroidery technique crafted from a vintage sari. “KPop Demon Hunters” star EJAE (@ejae_k) wore a stunning gown by Korean brand Leje (@leje.official) that included 1,000 pieces of mother-of-pearl and was inspired by peonies and the brushstrokes of an ink painting.
This year’s event celebrated the non-profit’s annual list of 100 most impactful Asian Pacific leaders across various industries. Chopra, actor @jetli, actor Charles Melton (@melton) as well as Olympic freestyle skier @eileengu were among those that picked up special honors.

"Seaworld Venice" has already captured the attention of many hundreds of people who lined up for a preview of the Austrian pavilion at this year's Venice Biennale.
The popular show by performance artist Florentina Holzinger and curator Nora-Swantje Almes asks viewers to rethink patriarchal systems "that currently control our lives," according to Almes.
More than half a million people are expected to visit the monumental international art fair, which opens to the public Saturday, over the next six months to see the work of over 100 artists and 99 nations, spread across 31 permanent national pavilions and a series of other exhibition spaces.

posts.caption:
Long before beauty tutorials and clean girl aesthetics, makeup was ritual, rebellion, theatre, status, protest. Ancient Egyptians painted their eyes almost like armor. In the 1920s, women used makeup as a symbol of freedom. And in fashion, the most interesting looks always came from people willing to look a little uncomfortable.
That’s why avant-garde makeup matters. The faceless skin, metallic bodies, distorted brows or surreal shapes aren’t supposed to feel safe, they’re supposed to make you stop and look. Fashion has always needed exaggeration, experimentation and a bit of madness to move forward. Otherwise everything starts looking the same.
Sometimes the best makeup look is the one that makes people ask: “wait… why does this make me feel weird?”

posts.caption:
Feathers have always existed somewhere between fashion and fantasy. Long before modern couture, they were symbols of power, status and spectacle, worn in royal courts, ceremonial dress and later transformed into pure glamour by Hollywood and haute couture. By the 1920s, feathers had already become one of fashion’s most dramatic signatures, moving with the body in a way no other material really can.
What makes feathers so timeless is how easily they shift moods. They can feel elegant, dangerous, theatrical or completely surreal depending on the designer, the styling or the photograph itself. That’s probably why fashion photography keeps returning to them. Feathers don’t just add texture, they create movement, shadow and emotion. They make clothes feel alive.
Looking at these images, you can see how different that language can become. Some looks feel sharp and almost intimidating, especially the darker silhouettes and oversized black feathers that turn the models into these sculptural, unreal characters. Others feel softer and exaggerated in a more playful way, like the pale feathered dresses that almost float against the white background. Even the bright red look feels less like clothing and more like performance.
Fashion constantly reinvents feathers, but the reason they survive never changes: they make people look twice.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINEⓇ on May 16, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'Say what you want, Feathers matter. CULT'.

posts.caption:
Szilveszter Makó is one of the few fashion photographers right now whose images feel instantly recognizable. The Hungarian born, Milan based photographer creates editorials that look less like traditional fashion shoots and more like fragments from a strange theatre play, an old folk tale, or a forgotten painting hanging in a museum somewhere. His work sits in this unusual space between fashion, performance art and set design, where every object feels handmade and every pose feels carefully rehearsed.
What makes Makó’s images stand out is how physical and constructed they feel. In an era where so much fashion photography is overly polished and digitally perfected, his work feels tactile and human. You can see the painted textures, handmade props and imperfect edges of the sets. Nothing feels sterile. Even the most surreal images still carry a certain warmth and intimacy.
A lot of his editorials feel like stage productions frozen into a single frame. Models are boxed into geometric spaces, surrounded by strange objects, dressed more like characters than fashion subjects. There are references to theatre, folklore, silent film and classical portraiture, but it never feels nostalgic in an obvious way. He uses those influences to build entirely new worlds around the clothes.
Light also plays a huge role in his work. Makó often avoids flash, which gives the photographs a softer, moodier atmosphere. His images feel cinematic without trying too hard. There is always tension inside them, even in the stillness.
What makes his photography so interesting from a fashion perspective is that the clothing never feels separate from the image itself. The garments become part of the story, part of the set, sometimes even part of the illusion. In a moment where so much fashion imagery starts looking the same, Makó’s work reminds people that editorials can still feel imaginative, theatrical and genuinely personal.

posts.caption:
In “Portrait of a Lady” for LOVE Magazine Spring/Summer 2016, Tim Walker pushes fashion photography into something stranger, more theatrical, and almost surreal. The editorial plays with exaggerated proportions, distorted femininity, heavy styling, and absurd details, yet never loses its elegance. Every image feels carefully constructed but still spontaneous, balancing humour, tension, and beauty at the same time.
What makes the story so compelling is the way it embraces excess without becoming chaotic. Oversized silhouettes, exaggerated bust shapes, eccentric props, and dramatic beauty looks turn the editorial into something between fashion fantasy and performance art. Inspired by the work of painter John Currin, Walker creates images that feel playful, slightly uncomfortable, and visually unforgettable.
The cast moves through these exaggerated scenes with complete seriousness, which gives the story its impact. Anna Cleveland, Eva Herzigova, Kiki Willems, Lexi Boling, Julie Hoomans, Molly Constable, and India Salvor Menuez each bring a different energy to the editorial, making every frame feel like its own strange character study.
Like much of Walker’s work, the story rejects minimalism completely. Instead, it celebrates imagination, visual storytelling, and the idea that fashion photography can still feel unexpected.

posts.caption:
“Fetching is Your Dior” from 1976 remains one of the boldest fashion campaigns ever produced.
Photographed by Chris von Wangenheim, the Dior jewelry advertisement captured model Lisa Taylor alongside a snarling Doberman, its jaws wrapped around her diamond covered wrist. The image was glamorous, provocative, and unsettling all at once, exactly the kind of visual language that began defining fashion photography in the second half of the 1970s.
Rather than presenting luxury as soft or untouchable, the campaign framed it as something sharper and more aggressive. Heavy jewelry, glossy makeup, sculpted hair, and dramatic poses gave the images a sense of control and confidence, while the presence of the dog introduced tension and unpredictability. It blurred the line between elegance and danger in a way that felt completely new at the time.
The campaign also reflected a larger shift happening in fashion imagery during the era. By the late 1970s, advertising was becoming more cinematic, sexually charged, and emotionally provocative. Photographers like Chris von Wangenheim moved away from traditional polished beauty photography and introduced darker, more psychological narratives that would go on to influence decades of fashion editorials and luxury campaigns.
Nearly fifty years later, “Fetching is Your Dior” still feels strikingly modern, not only because of its styling, but because of its attitude. It captured the moment fashion stopped trying to look merely beautiful and started wanting to look powerful.
Campaign Credits
Campaign: Fetching is Your Dior
Brand: Dior
Year: 1976
Photographer: Chris von Wangenheim
Model: Lisa Taylor

posts.caption:
Did you see Olivia Rodrigo’s ‘Drop Dead’ music video where she’s frolicking around Versailles wearing a Chloé blouse as pajamas? Did that look bother you?
For some viewers, the ‘babydoll’ look, owing to its name as well as its association with children’s apparel, called to mind the pedophilia which recently came to light in the Epstein Files. They’ve called Rodrigo’s look “baby cosplay” and have accused her of “normalising” pedophilic imagery.
But there are two outfits in ‘Drop Dead,’ one of which closely resembles a white Loris Azzaro dress formerly worn by Jane Birkin. This reference is important because Birkin wasn’t only iconic for wearing babydoll styles, but was a leader in a 60s style movement which freed women from cinched waists and allowed them the comfort of non-restrictive silhouettes. Perhaps Rodrigo anticipated the critique she is now receiving and was offering a Birkin as a counterpoint?
As well, Rodrigo confirmed that she found inspiration for her babydoll look — which she has doubled down on since the release of ‘Drop Dead’ — in such punky style mavens as Courtney Love and Kat Bjelland who wore the look in the 90s as a way to hold a middle finger up to establishment beauty standards. Their interpretation of the babydoll, which ran parallel to the riot grrrl movement and musicians like Kathleen Hanna and Kim Gordon, was dubbed “kinderwhore.”
Olivia Rodrigo, though, is a pop star whose work doesn’t compromise mainstream appeal despite channeling the ingredients of anti-establishment style. Can she pull off the “kinderwhore” look as a form of defiance? That looks to be the debate which reached a fever pitch this week when she wore another babydoll dress at Spotify’s Billions Club concert in Barcelona.
At its core, the debate is about the fine line between protecting women from exploitation and policing women’s autonomy, especially as it relates to the clothes they choose to wear. And with big pop-stars, this debate can be tricky; At what point are we oppressively scrutinizing what an intelligent, grown woman has chosen to wear, vs. at what point are we criticizing how the pop-culture icon has been styled for the sake of marketing?

posts.caption:
Tennessee Republicans just passed a new congressional map that redraws Memphis’ majority-Black Democratic district, a move critics say is designed to help the GOP keep control of the House.
Republicans claim the redistricting reflects population shifts, but opponents are calling BS, arguing the map is designed to dilute Black voting power.  “You cannot take a majority Black city, fracture its voting power and then tell us race has nothing to do with it,” Sen. London Lamar said.
The vote sparked major protests inside the state Capitol, with demonstrators filling the galleries alongside Democratic lawmakers. State Rep. Justin Jones burned a Confederate flag during the legislative session, while State Rep. Justin J. Pearson confronted THP troopers as they cleared protesters out. State Sen. Charlane Oliver stood on her desk holding a “No Jim Crow” banner before Republicans abruptly ended the session. Gov. Bill Lee signed the map into law shortly after.
The new districts come just days after SCOTUS weakened key Voting Rights Act protections, making Tennessee the first state to redraw congressional maps under the new ruling.

Eilish never thought she’d make another documentary after 2021’s ‘Billie Eilish: The World’s a Little Blurry,’ which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. “I’m so private about my actual life now,” Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‘Avatar’ filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up—and co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE’s May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: “I make art and music to reach people, to help people.” Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
When architect Jeanne Gang was hired to recreate the Samuel H. Scripps Theater Center for the Hudson Valley Shakespeare company, she had the chance to reconceive not only the theater group’s space, which was formerly a tent, but also the experience of both the actors and the audience. Gang, who is one of the most esteemed architects in the country, has always created work that serves many purposes—and designing theaters gets at the things she loves most about architecture.
“It’s like what Shakespeare himself said, ‘All the world’s a stage.’ I find I use principles of theater design in planning urban spaces, and for that matter, public space within a public building,” she says. “It’s like, where do people feel comfortable?”
Ahead of the opening of @hvshakespeare's new 14,850-square-foot theater, Gang spoke with ELLE about sustainability, the role that architecture can play in social justice, and the impact that a beautiful building can have on a community. Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
The planet is in trouble—and so is the fashion industry 😬⁠
⁠
As @apparelimpactinstitute found, a crash is coming in just a few quarters instead of decades. “Volatile carbon, energy and material markets are no longer theoretical,” the report explains. “They are shaping supplier pricing and operating costs across the apparel value chain.”⁠
⁠
The solutions for this aren’t some obscure, alien action but what our team of experts—and our peers, who have been pushing well over a decade for action—have been saying and saying and saying: better understanding their emissions, particularly in Scope 3, which includes raw materials and production.⁠
⁠
Working together, seeking transparency and justice, is a key to our collective resilience. The industry’s biggest brands must change too, pushing their peers and governments for change instead of cosying up with regimes that are destroying communities in the short and long term.  Learn more about this report at the link ↑⁠
⁠
#Sustainability #Fashion #Climate #Shopping #SheinGals⁠

posts.caption:
@shinyakozuka calling its latest @dickies pants “Baggy” barely covers it. ⁠
⁠
Regular Dickies 874s already lean loose, but Shinya Kozuka’s version pushes into full-blown JNCO territory. Still, in SHINYAKOZUKA’s world of exaggerated proportions and figure-swallowing silhouettes, these enormous pants feel perfectly on-brand. After years cultivating a cult following through conceptual runway shows and inflated tailoring, Kozuka is finally breaking beyond the niche of Japanese fashion diehards, helped by a recent LVMH Prize nomination, a Pitti Uomo runway debut, and growing viral attention online.⁠
⁠
The standout “Baggy” pants continue SHINYAKOZUKA’s long-running Dickies partnership, pairing ultra-wide proportions with sharp double pleats that give structure to all that volume. They’re part of a broader collection that twists familiar workwear into playful, experimental forms: pleated long shorts that almost resemble skirts, slouchy matching blazers, and balloon-shaped trousers built from darts and heavy fabric. The beauty of it all is how approachable it remains. SHINYAKOZUKA’s wild silhouettes are softened by classic Dickies materials, making the collection feel like an easy entry point into Kozuka’s wonderfully strange universe.

posts.caption:
The 2026 Fashion Show featured afternoon and evening runway presentations showcasing final collections from graduating students in @prattinstitute Fashion Department. Celebrating the school’s 125th annual showcase, this year’s collections explored identity, family, sustainability, and the connection between nature and technology, with many students focused on creating work that felt personal and meaningful. “It wasn't about something that was easy to reproduce. It was about making something that meant something,” said Korina Emmerich of EMME Studio, who received Pratt Institute’s 2026 Fashion Visionary Award during the evening show.⁠
⁠
Across 155 looks from 28 graduating designers, students presented menswear, womenswear, and unisex collections featuring weaving, embroidery, hand-dyeing, and other textile techniques inspired by personal stories and cultural traditions. Sustainability was also a major focus throughout the show, with students using responsibly sourced materials as part of Pratt’s Fashion BFA program. The department also honored graduating student Caleb Callahan with the 2026 Christopher Hunte “On Point” Award for Cork, Kerala, a collection exploring Irish and Indian anti-colonial histories through fashion and textile craft traditions.

posts.caption:
Old people just look cooler. Everything they wear gets an automatic boost, which is why fashion brands love casting senior models and why so much “good style” traces back to elderly uniforms anyway. I got reminded of this by a @beautifulpeople_officialsite x @neweracap campaign where an older guy in sunglasses and a leather jacket reading a newspaper looked infinitely cooler than the younger model doing the whole moody-paperback thing. No hate. Them’s the facts.⁠
⁠
The science is basically that everything looks better with some life in it. Older people have ease, confidence, reality. Wrinkles and gray hair aren’t flaws, they’re evidence of a life lived, which is way cooler than looking pristine and untouched anyway.

There's no better way to celebrate the arrival of the new NikeSKIMS collection than a session of pilates.
In an intimate launch event hosted by END., the fashion community came together in London to trial the latest pieces through a masterclass with expert Sophie Rose.
Designed with sculpting, intimates-inspired silhouettes and floral prints, the range delivered form and function while the popular NikeSKIMS split-toe rift offered a breathable footwear option to take them to and from the studio.
Guests also got a front row seat to a panel talk from Poppy Young, Leah Abbott and Isley Joseph which explored the evolution of pilates, style and performance as well as body confidence.
You can now shop the new NikeSKIMS drop online and in select END. stores, with an exclusive gift available for those purchasing in END. London.

posts.caption:
Art week is an endurance test and @esther.newyork wants to slow things down.
Co-founded by gallerists Margot Samel of @margotsamel.nyc and @temnikovakaselagallery’s Olga Temnikova, the indie fair doesn’t dream of being the next power player so much as bring as back to the simple pleasures of discovery, trading competition for collaboration in a thoughtful 22-gallery showcase.
Staged in Estonian House, the 19th-century Beaux-Arts gem, crosstown from the Frieze crowd, Esthers has earned a rep as art week’s intimate alternative, bringing forth a new vision for what fair can be.
As the indie favorite returns to Estonian House for one final run, Samel and Temnikova reflect on their stylish antidote to mega-fair spectacle. Tap the link in our bio for the full story.

posts.caption:
The quietest looks are always the loudest statements, and Avneet Kaur has known this for a while now. 🌸
Draped in the deep, unhurried cool of monochromatic indigo, she doesn’t chase the moment. She sets the pace for it. Denim-on-denim, lace against leather, coastal light and shadow, every element dialled into a frequency that’s entirely her own.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this isn’t just a fashion story. It’s a portrait of a generation that leads with composure, one that finds its power not in the loudest room, but in the stillest one. ✨
The industry moves fast. Avneet doesn’t rush. And somehow, she’s always exactly where the moment needs her to be.
Cool, collected, and completely in control, this is the indigo hour. And it belongs to her.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

The Y2K hits just keep coming. First popularized in the 1970s as a way to repurpose old jeans, the denim mini skirt truly hit its stride in the late ’90s and early aughts. Championed by millennium-era icons—from #BritneySpears and #ChristinaAguilera to #SiennaMiller and Cindy Crawford—the micro silhouette quickly became a pop-cultural mainstay. Though it ceded ground to mid-length hems in recent years, the denim mini has officially returned to our wardrobes just in time for the summer season.⁠

posts.caption:
For nearly four decades, Anna Wintour didn’t just lead Vogue, she reshaped the entire fashion industry.
From transforming magazine covers in the late 1980s to turning the Met Gala into fashion’s biggest global stage, Wintour built an influence that extended far beyond publishing. Known for her signature bob, dark sunglasses, and uncompromising standards, she became one of the most powerful figures in modern fashion.
Even after stepping down as Vogue’s editor-in-chief in 2025, Anna Wintour continues to lead as Condé Nast’s global editorial director, proving her impact on fashion and media remains unmatched. (biography.com)

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn’t just a romantic period drama, it’s one of Netflix’s most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show’s extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn’t stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show’s massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix
#Netflix #Luxurious

posts.caption:
From billionaire collectors to global athletes and celebrities, Jacob & Co. has built a reputation around creating some of the most extraordinary watches in modern horology.
Known for pushing boundaries through multi-axis tourbillons, gemstone-covered cases, moving automations, and collaborations inspired by hypercars, astronomy, and high jewellery, the brand has transformed ultra-watchmaking into spectacle. Pieces like the Billionaire, Astronomia, and Bugatti Chiron Tourbillon have helped Jacob & Co. stand apart in an industry traditionally dominated by heritage minimalism.
Now recognised as one of the fastest-growing Swiss watch brands, Jacob & Co. continues attracting a new generation of luxury buyers looking for exclusivity, engineering, and statement-making design all in one.

BMW has unveiled the Vision BMW Alpina Concept, offering the first real glimpse into Alpina’s future under full BMW ownership.
Revealed at the Concorso d’Eleganza Villa d’Este in Italy, the V8-powered grand tourer combines Alpina’s signature long-distance comfort with a sharper, more modern luxury design language. The concept features an elongated coupé silhouette, illuminated kidney grilles, classic Alpina detailing, and an ultra-refined four-seat interior focused on comfort and sophistication over outright aggression.
BMW says the concept previews a new era for Alpina as a standalone luxury sub-brand positioned between traditional BMW models and Rolls-Royce. The first production-era BMW Alpina model is expected to arrive next year, likely inspired by the 7 Series platform.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.

Jonathan Anderson unveiled his first Dior cruise show — and first coed one — at the recently reopened and reinvented Los Angeles County Museum of Art on Wednesday evening.
Miley Cyrus, LaKeith Stanfield, Anya Taylor-Joy and Al Pacino were there, ogling Anderson’s take on L.A.’s dream factory, complete with vintage cars, its own pseudo screenplay, an Ed Ruscha collaboration and a signal that this may be the beginning of a new relationship between Dior and the film world. (Anderson has, after all, been the costume designer of a number of movies, including “Challengers.”) Our fashion critic, Vanessa Friedman, was watching from afar, but the drama was impossible to miss.
There was old silver-screen glamour, updated for the 21st century. Ripped jeans got a new twist by incorporating filament-thin silver chains amid the distressed cotton. Accessories, like Philip Treacy hats with the words “Dior” and “Star” on the top, bejeweled bags and chain-mail earrings snagged the big cameos. And florals were abundant. Tap the link in our bio to read @vvfriedman’s full review of the @dior cruise collection. Photos by Dior.

posts.caption:
The days of giving pharmaceutical-branded swag, like like pens, mugs and even candleholders, to health care professionals are largely over. Now those items are hot on the thrifting scene.
Pharmacore — medical-branded pieces worn as fashion — has found new expression at the confluence of identity, medicine and commerce, and at a time when skepticism toward pharmaceuticals is at a high. But it's not just the vintage pieces that are finding fans. Since 2021, the Etsy shop Dr. Sign has sold more than 8,000 items featuring names and logos like Ozempic and Zoloft.
Preston Roche, a psychiatry resident and pharma swag collector, said such garments appeal for many reasons. “We’re almost mocking Big Pharma and joking about capitalism in medicine,” he said. "“Or maybe that’s just me.”
At the link in our bio, read more of the rise of pharmacore and see photos of the items vintage shoppers are seeking. Photos by Evie Hennick, Preston Roche, Gabriel Grais

posts.caption:
You want to know where men’s fashion is heading? Follow the geeks. These are the obsessives, fixated on how their pants fit, who were early to selvage denim and soft-shouldered Italian tailoring.
And where has the attention of this cohort landed now? On a vanguard of newish-to-the-West labels from Japan like A.Presse, Comoli, Auralee and T.T.
Basic though these clothes appear, their hook is that they’re opulent to the touch, elevated in their fabrication. The sudden popularity of these labels outside Japan can make it feel as if they are new. Yet each label has built a respectable business within Japan, some for more than a decade.
And they have swiftly wiggled into the broader fashion community. "While I was in Paris for the men’s fashion shows a year ago January, all anyone wanted to talk about were things with a 'Made in Japan' tag," @jacobwgallagher writes.
At the link in our bio, read more about the Japanese brands that are becoming the most talked about thing in men's fashion. Visuals by @pablodeclan, @danielledelplato, A.Presse, Auralee, ssstein

posts.caption: For all those that do not work in the apparel industry let me explain...white pants are very often worn in summer. Summer is usually warm...so pants with lining are too hot to wear. Now for those that say make them in thicker fabric - even when you double the weight youll still see pocket bags. We try different things to make the pockets less visible, we cut them in lightweight fabric so theyre more sheer, we cut them in nude lining etc.. and none of it works. The onky thing that does work is no pockets. Now we walk around with crop tops and vast areas of skin on display but heaven forbid we see legs through those pants! Good grief

posts.caption:
Planning a wedding can feel overwhelming, but The New York Times Wedding section is here to help. Whether you are eloping at city hall, hosting an intimate garden celebration or planning a destination getaway, we have tips on how to manage everything.

posts.caption:
One of my favourite signatures of #SarahBurton, and something I’m excited to see her continue at #Givenchy is her extraordinary couture fringe technique. First fully realised for #AlexanderMcQueen Fall 2018, she drew inspiration from insects, butterflies, and metamorphosis to create the  butterfly-wing gown, dissolving into cascading silk threads.
These garments are so fascinating because of the level of craftsmanship required. The threads are dyed, blended, layered, and hand-applied to create subtle gradients of colour and movement. I had the chance to see one IRL and the delicacy of the work is unbelievable. The fringe requires constant brushing and arrangement, with even the smallest movement causing the threads to tangle, making the garments feel almost alive.

First appearing in the #Valentino Fall 2010/11 runway collection, the Rockstud became one of the most recognisable shoes of the decade, becoming a defining visual code for the house. Now they return, reimagined with a new silhouette, cage design, and chiselled toe finished with a cap.

posts.caption:
In honour of #MiucciaPrada’s 77th birthday, let’s look back at one of the most beautiful examples of movie costume design - @bazluhrmann’s 2013 #TheGreatGatsby.
Working alongside @catherinemartindesigns , Miuccia and her team adapted 40 #Prada and #MiuMiu dresses from the archives for the film’s party scenes. Creating modern interpreation of the jazz age rather than historically accurate 1920s costumes. The garments were made with crystals, feathers, and fringe, chosen not only for their beauty but for the way they moved on screen.
Following the film’s release, an exhibition was held in New York and Tokyo to showcase the costumes designed for the movie. The dresses later reappeared in 2021 when @lorenzoposocco pulled several pieces from the archive for #DuaLipa “We’re Good” music video, including the infamous chandelier dress.

posts.caption:
Years ago, two strangers met at a common friend's wedding. In the middle of all the energy and chaos that Indian weddings bring, they found themselves craving a warm, soothing cup of tea and ordered it straight to the wedding venue. Thus, a serendipitous friendship was formed. What followed were regular catch-ups, long conversations about tea, and travels spent collecting blends from around the world.
No, this isn't a movie plot. This is the story of Deepthi Srinivas @deeflies, a pilot by profession, and Monica HS @hs.mon, a tech entrepreneur, who now turn their love for tea into curated high-tea events in Bengaluru.
They are the founders of The Tea Soiree @theteasoiree through which they host intimate gatherings for people to experience different kinds of tea, savour the beauty of slow moments, and form unexpected bonds like they once did.
“Bengaluru has become very fast-paced, and we wanted to create a space where people can escape this rushed environment and just sit down, relax, and learn more about tea,” Monica tells me.
Deepthi adds, "We haven't professionally learnt about tea, but we love to share our collection and host tea parties amongst our friends; so we thought, why not extend that to other guests as well?"
Read full interview by Tanya Savkoor at www.shethepeople.tv

Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) has been renamed to polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS), according to an announcement on Tuesday during the European Congress of Endocrinology.
According to the researchers, the term “polycystic ovary syndrome” frequently led patients and healthcare workers to associate the disorder primarily with ovarian cysts, even though many women diagnosed with the condition do not actually develop cysts.
The name polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome is intended to better capture the condition's complex impact on the body.

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

posts.caption:
A fashion stylist learns most of this career the hard way.
The first five years are low pay, sometimes no pay. You will carry samples in the rain more times than you can count. Your friends might not understand what you do.
People think your life is glamorous when it is not. The work is lonely. You will be the only one who sees the faults in every image.
The rate conversation never gets easier. And the career compounds in a way you only see when you turn around.
Nobody hands you the manual. You learn the job by living it.
✦ The career is real. It just looks nothing like what you imagined when you started. ✦
Anyone three years in knows what this is. Anyone ten years in has lived every line on this list.
💚 For every fashion stylist still in their first five years, deciding whether to keep going
💚 For every stylist who looked at the rain and the bag and almost left
💚 For every working fashion stylist who stayed long enough to see the career start compounding

Returns are part of the job. The rate is what it is for a reason. And we want the job to be good as much as you do.

posts.caption:
There is a point where jewellery stops behaving like ornament and starts asking to be read. New York-based label Eskae (@eskae.atelier) sits in that space, creating sculptural pieces that challenge the polite expectations of modern jewellery. “I was inspired to start ESKAE by a desire to create jewellery that holds meaning beyond adornment… ESKAE is a play on the suffix ‘esque’, suggesting the essence of something rather than a fixed definition. I wanted to build a brand that feels like a state of being rather than a label, something that invites interpretation,” says founder Drishti Verma (@drishtivermaa).
Born in India and now based in New York City, Verma works between two cultural registers. India’s layered traditions and New York’s global landscape have shaped her understanding of “form, meaning and universality and how intimate belief systems can be shared and understood across different contexts.” In her work, that duality appears through contrast: structure and fluidity, restraint and expression, silence and intensity.
Drawing from mythology, philosophy and psychology, Eskae is anchored in what Verma calls the “unspoken”: emotional states that exist before language. The result is jewellery that feels sculptural, intimate and alive.

posts.caption:
In India, guilt has rarely been just a feeling. It sits inside silence, duty, family expectation, internalised shame and the promise of karmic correction.
Now it has another life online. A post, a reshare, a hashtag or the right therapy-speak can start to look like social accountability. Sometimes it is care. Sometimes it is performance. Often, it is both.
Privilege still decides who gets to look away. Women and marginalised groups are often left carrying the heaviest moral burden, while Gen Z is learning to draw boundaries and inherit a new guilt through visibility, outrage and the pressure to post.
What guilt reveals is less about goodness and more about power.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#GuiltInIndia #InternalisedGuilt #SocialAccountability #FamilyBoundaries #performativeactivism

posts.caption:
Favre Leuba’s revival begins where serious watchmaking often does: in the archive.
Founded in 1737, the maison’s record runs from frontier instruments to formal complications. In 1962, it launched the Bivouac, the first mechanical watch with an aneroid barometer. In 1968 came the Bathy, the first mechanical watch with a depth gauge. Altitude, depth and expedition were not decorative ideas for the brand. They were functions.
That is why its Watches and Wonders Geneva 2026 debut matters beyond the fair calendar. Less than two years after its 2024 revival, Favre Leuba entered the conversation with two watches drawn from its own past: the Harpoon Revival and the 1737 Triple Calendar.
The archive also sits close to high watchmaking. Favre Leuba’s name has appeared on dials alongside Patek Philippe, Zenith, IWC and Jaeger-LeCoultre, including the Calatrava by Patek Philippe. In 1948, the maison also acquired Bovet’s name and manufacturing facilities, producing high-precision chronographs under the Favre Leuba Bovet name.
For India, the story runs deeper. Favre Leuba became the first Swiss watch brand to enter the country in 1865, with its first Indian store opening in Bombay. A Zenith and Favre Leuba co-stamped pocket watch, gifted to Mahatma Gandhi by Jawaharlal Nehru, is still on display at the Gandhi National Museum in New Delhi.
Some revivals feel inevitable. Favre Leuba’s feels like the archive moving again.

posts.caption:
Illustrated journalism has always lived in the margins of serious news, despite doing some of its sharpest work.
In India, it has appeared as famine sketches, political cartoons and graphic testimony, often capturing what photographs or straight copy could not.
In 2026, that form received one of journalism’s most visible validations. Anand Radhakrishnan’s trAPPed, a Bloomberg graphic story on a digital arrest scam in India, won the Pulitzer Prize for Illustrated Reporting and Commentary, shared with journalists Suparna Sharma and Natalie Obiko Pearson.
The story follows Dr Ruchika Tandon, a Lucknow neurologist held under digital control for eight days by scammers using video calls, staged authority and sound effects. With few images to lean on, trAPPed turns fear, surveillance and psychological control into reportage.
In a media climate where India ranks 157 out of 180 on the World Press Freedom Index, the drawn frame feels less like a stylistic choice and more like a serious journalistic instrument.
Link in bio
By Arshia Dhar (@urseaa)
#illustratedjournalism #PulitzerPrize #visualreportage #Indianmedia #cultureandmedia

posts.caption:
Absolut Creative Commune, Edition Four, gathers pace without fixing a single direction. Curated by SPRYK (@spryk.in), Absolut Creative Commune (@absolutborncolourless) brings together Abhijit Vinayak (@abhijitvinayak), Akshita Sinha (@akshitasnha), Amalendu Kaushik (@amalendu_k), Damini Gupta (@daminope), Era Namjoshi (@eranamjoshi), and Surabhi Banerjee (@surabhi_b6), each arriving with their own references, methods, and tensions.
Set against a moment where images are increasingly seamless and uniform, this edition leans into difference as a condition. The works don’t yield a single reading. They hold onto friction, authorship, and intent without smoothing themselves out.
The result stays open, shaped as much by process as by what finally comes into view.

posts.caption:
Another major shift in the luxury landscape: LVMH has officially agreed to sell its majority stake in Marc Jacobs to a 50/50 joint venture between WHP Global and G-III Apparel Group. The deal reportedly values the brand at around $850 million, bringing an end to LVMH’s nearly 30-year relationship with the house.
Importantly, Marc Jacobs himself is staying on as Creative Director, continuing to oversee the brand’s vision, runway collections, and overall creative direction.
The move feels very reflective of where fashion is right now: heritage brands increasingly operating at the intersection of luxury image, licensing power, and large-scale global manufacturing.
A business headline, yes, but also a significant industry turning point.
#LVMH #MarcJacobs #FashionIndustry #LuxuryFashion #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
So… what did we all think of Jonathan Anderson’s first Dior Cruise collection? Presented last night beneath the concrete arches of David Geffen Galleries at LACMA, the show leaned heavily into cinematic atmosphere: vintage cars, noir lighting, and models emerging slowly into focus.
The collection itself mixed fluttering dresses, glinting tweeds, feathered headpieces, and unraveling Bar jackets with more casual elements like denim-inspired pieces and crisp men’s shirting featuring artwork by Ed Ruscha. It felt polished in some moments and intentionally undone in others.
One of the strongest references came through Anderson revisiting a white Bar jacket originally created by Christian Dior for Marlene Dietrich during Stage Fright—tying the collection back into Dior’s longstanding relationship with costume and cinema.
It’s coming up on a year now since his debut… has it been a good one?
#Dior #JonathanAnderson #DiorCruise #FashionReview #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
If you love @oliviadeano’s concert style right now, you need to meet @cliopeppiatt. Her custom looks for The Art of Loving tour are basically a masterclass in modern stage dressing—heavily embellished, hyper-detailed, and covered in intricate beadwork that feels nostalgic without reading costume-y. There are clear references to icons like Cher and Tina Turner, but filtered through this softer, London-girl lens that still feels very current.
What’s amazing is how far the brand’s reach has stretched beyond the stage even before Olivia. Bella Hadid and Alexandra Saint Mleux have both embraced Peppiatt’s signature sparkle-heavy aesthetic, bringing it into completely different style worlds.
And yes, photographer and art director @szilvesztermako just shot the campaign for the brand’s new bridal collection, which honestly feels like a perfect match creatively.
Very dreamy. Very embellished. Very “main character leaving the party at 2AM.”
#ClioPeppiatt #OliviaDean #BellaHadid #SzilveszterMako #FashionDesign

The new Apple of Chloé’s eye is here! Apple Martin is officially fronting the house’s new high-summer campaign, Chloé à la Plage 2026, marking her first major collaboration with the brand. Directed by Chemena Kamali and photographed by David Sims, the campaign leans fully into that soft, dreamlike Chloé universe—sun-faded femininity, instinctive styling, and the kind of romantic summer imagery that feels half memory, half fantasy.
Everything feels light, effortless, and just a little surreal. A very strong first major fashion moment for her.

posts.caption:
Working in fashion is often sold as a nonstop runway show, but the reality is that a lot of the industry runs on spreadsheets, deadlines, logistics, and an almost unbelievable level of consumption fatigue. It’s strange when your professional life revolves around identifying “the next big thing” while your personal brain is quietly begging for less noise.
Once you peel back the glamour, you realize most fashion jobs are actually about problem-solving. Managing chaos. Translating ideas into systems people can execute. The “unglamorous” parts—production timelines, budgets, analytics, inventory, endless inboxes—are usually where the real skill lives.
Anyone can love clothes. Not everyone can handle the machinery behind them. Happy Monday to the ones who do.
#FashionIndustry #FashionCareers #FashionWeek #TheCitizensPoste #MoodBoard

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Growing up in Chennai as the youngest of three siblings, acting wasn’t part of the plan for model student Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). During her gap year after college, the then 21-year-old dabbled in modelling and ended up at a film audition for kicks. “I thought if I do get it, I’ll do it for a year and then go back to studying,” she says. “The idea was to do it temporarily, never make it a career. I’m sure my teachers were disappointed when I became an actor. I think some of them still are,” she laughs.
We’re not the first to ask her what her alternative career would be, but this time she has an answer: “I’m doing it now,” she says with excitement. “I think this entrepreneurial journey is what I’ve always wanted to do,” says the serial entrepreneur, whose work straddles films, beauty, wellness, fashion, food, and sport, all at once.
For our second anniversary issue, @chloechou13 flew to Hyderabad to chat with our cover star. Full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Samantha Ruth Prabhu’s (@samantharuthprabhuoffl) face card is immaculate. We notice this on a Friday morning, when we are on the set of our May cover shoot in Mumbai, and the star is getting ready for her first shot. At some point, the topic moves on to her upcoming film, and someone in the room asks, “Who’s the hero?” Samantha simply says, “I don’t do those kinds of films anymore.” We can’t help but grin.
It’s been a busy seven days for the star, between promotions and shooting patchwork for her upcoming film ‘Maa Inti Bangaaram’. “The story will be the hero in the movies that I do. The story is bigger than the producer, bigger than the actors in it.”
It isn’t about occupying the lead role on the top of the call sheet, she explains. In her career, Samantha has, in several of her biggest films, played characters orbiting male superstars, and knows all too well that female characters are often relegated to the role of a catalyst in the male protagonist’s journey, to propel his emotional arc as a narrative device. She, however, counterbalanced them with more textured roles, such as the journalist and storyteller Madhuravani in ‘Mahanati’ (2018), the emotionally complex Vaembu in the cult neo-noir ‘Super Deluxe’ (2019), and the 70-year-old trapped in a younger body as Swathi in ‘Oh! Baby’ (2019).

posts.caption:
For our second anniversary issue, there was no looking beyond Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). Two years ago, she almost quit acting when she was diagnosed with myositis, an autoimmune condition that causes muscle weakness, fatigue, and pain. It forced her to slow down and taught her a lesson at peak fame that she will never forget. “I was just so focused on running this race for which I don’t even know what the prize at the finish line was,” recalls the actor of the time when everything came to a grinding halt.
Since then, she’s renegotiated everything: her work ethic, priorities, even brand deals. “I realised all the checklists that I was so eager to tick off weren’t my own. They were checklists made for me by other people,” she says. Now at 39, she’s not only a top-billed actor but also an audacious producer, a serial entrepreneur, a fierce investor, and a chatty podcast host. At the link in bio, the multi-hyphenate talks to @chloechou13 about success, silent retreats, swimming, and what is likely to be her joyful next chapter.

posts.caption:
The world over, a luxury business hotel has come to mean just one thing: a bland, uniform glass edifice that opens to beige rooms, pared-down interiors and clean white linens. It’s comfortable to stay, with a service that is mostly immaculate, but it doesn’t leave you with the intimacy of a boutique hotel that makes you feel at home.
But Roswyn (@roswynhotel)—Ennismore’s first Morgans Originals address in India (and the fourth around the world) proves otherwise.
Just minutes from Mumbai’s international airport, this all-suite hotel, which shares a wall with Fairmont, has 109 rooms, available as 1 or 2BHK spaces. Within this apartment-like layout is a kitchenette, a dedicated work area with touchscreen TV, and even a bar stocked with cocktail blends. Inside the bath, there’s Le Labo toiletries that are worth bringing back and Nespresso pods that will remind you of home. All this is to confirm that it carries the kind of swagger and soul that you almost never expect from a boring business hotel.
At the link in bio, @tanviii_05 with a full review of the maximalist hotel.

Masoom Minawala (@masoomminawala)is no stranger to the Cannes Film Festival red carpet. Over the years, she’s worn several big names including Manish Malhotra, Abu Jani and Sandeep Khosla, and Arpita Mehta. But for her seventh year on the Croisette, instead of just turning to India’s marquee couture names, the entrepreneur and investor also decided to spotlight younger designers who could benefit from the visibility of a global stage.
For the premiere of ‘sHEALed’ today, Minawala wore a custom crochet look by emerging knitwear designer Anushka Sanghvi (@anushka.sanghvi)—who stylist Dolla Baruah (@dollaypop) had first discovered on Instagram.
While crochet has been a recurring element across runways this season, Baruah was drawn to the craft for its slowness and intimacy. “Every loop is built by hand, one after another, and that patience felt powerful for a red carpet usually dominated by instant spectacle,” she adds.
And it is this slow technique that sits at the heart of Sanghvi’s practice.
At the link in bio @chloechou13 chats with Baruah and Sanghvi on how the look came together.
Photography: @densengraphy

posts.caption:
This is not a lecture on fiscal virtue. But fashion, historically, is rarely good at recognising when the mood changes. And in India, the mood seems to be shifting. Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s recent SpendSmart call for economic patriotism—buy less, buy wisely, postpone foreign holidays, suspend non-essential gold purchases, choose India-made products and even “wed in India”—arrives at a moment when India is showing off spectacular consumption. The Met Gala “wealth and style” discourse has barely subsided when paid-for visibility at Cannes and chatter around forthcoming couture weddings and shows is surging. Even Indian pret is on its own trip—garments are priced anywhere between ₹20,000 and ₹60,000.
Designers insist that all is well; even shirts priced upwards of ₹30,000 sell. Luxury managers and marketers, however, have a different refrain: all is “in” the well.
If India takes SpendSmart seriously—and signs suggest it already is—fashion’s current excess may begin to look oddly out of step. Reports of companies curbing foreign travel, conserving energy, oil and resources already point to a recalibration.
Fashion’s mood, though, appears entirely contrary. At Cannes, even aspirant celebrities reportedly pay staggering sums to walk the red carpet and be photographed. Across fashion and celebrity culture, there is an almost stubborn insistence that nothing is wrong with the world. Even when geopolitical instability is rattling markets, foreign investment remains unsettled and the rupee has weakened sharply against the dollar.
If India begins applauding strategic spending, will fashion—which is by nature capitalist and aspirational—begin to appear tone-deaf in all its excesses?
Which raises the question. Is economic patriotism merely about buying and wedding in India? Or will SpendSmart demand something tougher: downsizing lavishness and embracing strategic restraint? Fashion survives on visibility and money. It sells fantasy for a living. If the cultural mood shifts toward prudence, will fashion’s business model be compromised if it tones itself down?
In this current economic mood, what might a middle path look like? Tell us what you think.
🖋️@shefatwork

posts.caption:
Right to Be Seen Travels to London Craft Week
The Right to Be Seen: Reframing the Passport Photo in Craft, conceptualised by The Voice of Fashion, created in collaboration with Royal Enfield Social Mission and curated by Juhi Pandey, has now travelled to the London Craft Week. Presented as part of Royal Enfield’s The Himalayan Knot showcase at the The Friday Sari Project in Hampstead, the broader project reframes identity, visibility and representation through the lens of handmade practice.
At the London showcase, visitors will encounter portraits of artisan Raziya Bano from Kargil, whose work offers a meditation on labour and belonging in the Himalayan region. Originally exhibited in December 2025 at Journeying Across the Himalayas, Royal Enfield’s multidisciplinary cultural festival in Delhi, the project now travels to an international audience at a moment when conversations around craft are increasingly tied to authorship, ethics and recognition.
The Right to Be Seen series asks a simple question: what happens when artisans create their own portraits using the very skills that define their craft traditions? Built around the idea of the identity photograph, the project invites makers to reinterpret their own faces through embroidery, weaving and textile-based hand techniques, shifting them from anonymous custodians of craft to visible authors of culture.
Part exhibition, part textile conservation initiative, The Himalayan Knot brings together capsule collections shaped by indigenous knowledge systems and contemporary design language. Among the highlights are The Fifth Gesture, created by label MARGN with the women artisans of self-help group Jomche in Himachal Pradesh and Higher Plains and Suket Dhir’s collaboration with Looms of Ladakh, which merges pashmina with jamdani techniques in earthy shades drawn from Indian madder and marigold.
🖋️ @anannya_s_

posts.caption: You cleverly use progressive language but without viewing things through the lens of class, it stops from being a true feminist discussion. Alia is out there wearing processes couture a day after Indians were told to tighten their belts, to brace for hardships, to not use petrol, to refrain from travelling. She boarded a flight and went t Europe when Indians were asked not to fly. It’s like there is a different set of rules for the hyper privileged and a different India for them. It’s not restrained glamour at all - it’s laughing at the have-nots. It’s exploitative. It’s hiding behind girl-boss language but never expressing class solidarity. When Indians like Alia got on such global stages - exactly who are they representing? The other elites? Or, are they representing the struggling masses who can’t afford the most basic necessities. Fashion without context is just colours. Feminism without intersectionality is nothing more than an excuse for bad behavior.

Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.

Fabric and Accessories

Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.

posts.caption:
My married surname is Tucker. I’ve been using it for years without knowing what it meant.
Tucker comes from the Old English tucian which means to torment or beat cloth. A tucker was a cloth finisher: someone who took raw woven fabric off the loom and scoured it, beat it, trampled it in water until it became finished cloth ready to wear.
It was skilled physical labour that was essential to the entire textile trade.
The same job had three names depending on where you were in England. Fuller in the north. Walker in Scotland and the Midlands. Tucker in the West Country…Devon, Somerset, Wiltshire, Dorset, Cornwall.
My husband’s patrilineal family came from Plymouth and Cornwall.
The name came from exactly the place where it originated, where the cloth trade shaped the landscape and the language for centuries.
And Cornwall isn’t simply the far end of England, Kernow is one of the six Celtic nations with a textile tradition that predates the Norman conquest by centuries.
I am a textile and fashion designer and I married into the name of a cloth worker from a Celtic textile region without knowing any of this.
This beautiful man and his name found me 🫶🏼😭
Tucker is one surname and there are dozens more carrying the same secret and I’m going deep on more of them in the next episode of my podcast, The Culture of Cloth 🎧 Link in bio.
